title: Caco-2 Cells for Measuring Intestinal Cholesterol Transport - Possibilities and Limitations
author: Hiebl, Schachner, et al.
description: BackgroundThe human Caco-2 cell line is a common in vitro model of the intestinal epithelial barrier. As the intestine is a major interface in cholesterol turnover and represents a non-biliary pathway for cholesterol excretion, Caco-2 cells are also a valuable model for studying cholesterol homeostasis, including cholesterol uptake and efflux. Currently available protocols are, however, either sketchy or not consistent among different laboratories. Our aim was therefore to generate a collection of optimized protocols, considering the different approaches of the different laboratories and to highlight possibilities and limitations of measuring cholesterol transport with this cell line.ResultsWe developed comprehensive and quality-controlled protocols for the cultivation of Caco-2 cells on filter inserts in a single tight monolayer. A cholesterol uptake as well as a cholesterol efflux assay is described in detail, including suitable positive controls. We further show that Caco-2 cells can be efficiently transfected for luciferase reporter gene assays in order to determine nuclear receptor activation, main transcriptional regulators of cholesterol transporters (ABCA1, ABCB1, ABCG5/8, NPC1L1). Detection of protein and mRNA levels of cholesterol transporters in cells grown on filter inserts can pose challenges for which we highlight essential steps and alternative approaches for consideration. A protocol for viability assays with cells differentiated on filter inserts is provided for the first time.ConclusionsThe Caco-2 cell line is widely used in the scientific community as model for the intestinal epithelium, although with highly divergent protocols. The herein provided information and protocols can be a common basis for researchers intending to use Caco-2 cells in the context of cellular cholesterol homeostasis.

steps_raw.title: Transfection of Caco-2 Cells and Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay
steps_raw.instruction:
Several nuclear receptors have crucial implications in the regulation of cholesterol homeostasis (reviewed in [18, 19]). Determining the activation of distinct nuclear receptors is therefore of high interest in the field of cholesterol and atherosclerosis research. A wide-spread assay used to investigate nuclear receptor activation is the luciferase reporter gene assay, which is usually carried out in HEK293 cells [20], as they are easy to transfect. Nonetheless, (organ) specific cellular features like the expression of co-regulatory proteins that affect gene expression may be unique in intestinal Caco-2 cells (reviewed in [21]). We, therefore, decided to transfect Caco-2 cells and to establish a protocol for a luciferase reporter gene assay in this cell line (Protocol #3). Since differentiated cells are far more resistant to transfection [22], undifferentiated cells were used for transfection and the subsequent luciferase reporter gene assay. Several groups already successfully transfected Caco-2 cells, however, the used transfection reagents differed between these groups. We found publications using FuGENE® 6, Lipofectamine™ 2000, calcium phosphate coprecipitation, PEI (polyethylenimine) and other methods, including different nanoparticles for improved gene delivery [23–27]. We, therefore, decided to test several transfection reagents. From all tested transfection reagents, i.e. FuGENE® HD, the calcium phosphate precipitation method [20, 28] and LPEI (linear polyethylenimine), only Lipofectamine™ LTX led to an acceptable transient transfection efficiency as judged by the green fluorescence provided by the internal control EGFP. We then adapted the protocol of the manufacturer of Lipofectamine™ LTX regarding the number of cells seeded, the amount of Lipofectamine™ LTX used per well and the ratios of the expression, reporter and internal control plasmids transfected. With the cell seeding density recommended in our protocol, approximately 50% confluence is reached when transfection is started, which we found appropriate to achieve a high transfection efficiency and to avoid excessive cell death. In general, high confluence is associated with a reduced transfection efficiency, but a low seeding number can result in increased cell death during transfection [29]. We co-transfected the cells with a human full-length LXRα or LXRβ expression plasmid, the firefly luciferase reporter plasmid ABCA1_Luc and an EGFP expression plasmid, which served as an internal control, in a ratio of 1:3:1. For the mammalian one-hybrid assay, cells were transiently transfected with an expression plasmid encoding a chimeric protein consisting of the yeast Gal4-DBD fused in frame to the hLXRα/β-LBD, a reporter plasmid containing specific upstream activating sequences (UAS)_Luc, and the EGFP expression plasmid, which again served as internal control, in a ratio of 5:3:1. Fig. 2a-d shows the results of cells treated with increasing concentrations of the LXR agonist GW3965. Luciferase activity was significantly induced in a dose-dependent manner, except for hLXRβ, which showed a plateau from 1 μM GW3965 upwards. Altogether, the results show that Caco-2 cells can be efficiently transfected for luciferase reporter gene assays using Lipofectamine™ LTX.
Fig. 2a–d Luciferase Reporter Gene Assays with Caco-2 cells, applying Protocol #3. Caco-2 cells were transiently co-transfected with a human full-length LXRα (a) or LXRβ (b) expression plasmid, the firefly luciferase reporter plasmid ABCA1_Luc and an EGFP expression plasmid, in a ratio of 1:3:1, using Lipofectamine™ LTX. In the case of the mammalian one-hybrid assays, cells were transiently transfected with an expression plasmid encoding a chimeric protein consisting of the yeast Gal4-DBD fused in frame to the hLXRα-LBD (c) or to the hLXRβ-LBD (d), a reporter plasmid containing specific upstream activating sequences (UAS)_Luc, and the EGFP expression plasmid, in a ratio of 5:3:1. Cells were then treated with GW3965 (LXR agonist, 0.1–10 μM) or the vehicle control (DMSO 0.1%). The luciferase-derived luminescence was normalized to the EGFP-derived fluorescence, and set in relation to the solvent vehicle. Bar graphs represent mean ± SD from three independent experiments each performed in quadruplicate. ns not significant, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001 versus solvent vehicle (determined by one-way ANOVA with Dunnett post-test)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS, complete growth medium and trypsin/EDTA for 30 min in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Pour off the medium and discard it.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Rinse the cells with 12 ml PBS and discard it. Note: Volumes are given for 75 cm2 flasks!

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Add 3 ml trypsin/EDTA to the flask and incubate for 5 min in an incubator at 37 °C.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Knock to the sides of the flask to detach still adherent cells and then add 7 ml of complete growth medium to neutralize the trypsin/EDTA solution.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Gently pipet up and down to separate cells and avoid cell clumps.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Remove 1 ml of the cell suspension for cell counting and viability measurement.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Transfer 1 × 106 cells (Monday) or 0.5 × 106 cells (Thursday) to a new 75 cm2 flask and fill up with complete growth medium to a total volume of 15 ml. Note: Using these cell densities enables subculturing only twice per week (Monday and Thursday). However, medium has to be renewed every other day (3 times per week).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #1: Subculturing of Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: We use Caco-2 cells between in-house passage numbers 5 to 20 only.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: Seeding of Caco-2 cells onto filter inserts is preferably done on Thursdays. Translucent inserts should be used, except for confocal laser scanning microscopy and viability assays where transparent inserts are required. Note: Volumes are given for 12-well plates with inserts from Sarstedt (growth area = 1.1 cm2).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS, complete growth medium and trypsin/EDTA for 30 min in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Pour off the medium and discard it.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Rinse the cells with 12 ml PBS and discard it. Note: Volumes are given for 75 cm2 flasks!

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Add 3 ml trypsin/EDTA to the flask and incubate for 5 min in an incubator at 37 °C.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Knock to the sides of the flask to detach still adherent cells and then add 7 ml of complete growth medium to neutralize the trypsin/EDTA solution.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Gently pipet up and down to separate cells and avoid cell clumps.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Remove 1 ml of the cell suspension for cell counting and viability measurement.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Pre-wet the filter inserts and wells with complete growth medium for at least 2 min. Volumes used are: apical (insert) 0.5 ml and basolateral (well) 1.6 ml.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 9. One insert has to be cultivated without cells, since this is required for the measurement of the TEER (transepithelial electrical resistance) blank value. Note: Consult Supplementary Protocol #3 (Additional file 1) for further information on TEER measurement.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 10. Prepare the cell suspension required for seeding in complete growth medium. 0.06 × 106 cells/cm2 should be seeded onto the filter inserts in a volume of 0.5 ml.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 11. Aspirate the medium in the inserts you want to seed the cells onto.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 12. Seed cells into the inserts and gently shake the plate to evenly distribute cells.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 13. Put the plate into an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 14. Change the medium 3 h after seeding to basolateral medium, both in the insert and the well.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #2: Cultivation of Caco-2 Cells on Filter Inserts – Spontaneous Differentiation
steps_raw.instruction: 15. Again change the medium the next day to avoid multilayer formation. Hereafter, medium has to be renewed every other day (3 times per week) till the end of the cultivation period, which is usually after 19–21 days. Note: Caco-2 cells are grown under symmetric conditions (i.e. basolateral medium in both the filter insert and the well) for 7 days. Change to asymmetric conditions on day 7 of cultivation (i.e. apical medium in the filter insert, basolateral medium in the well). Apical medium is serum-free, whereas basolateral medium contains 20% FBS. This resembles more closely the physiological conditions.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: Day 1 afternoon: (e.g. Monday 15:00)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS, complete growth medium and trypsin/EDTA in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Pour off the medium and discard it.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Rinse the cells with 12 ml PBS and discard it. Note: Volumes are given for 75 cm2 flasks!

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Add 3 ml trypsin/EDTA to the flask and incubate for 5 min in an incubator at 37 °C.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Knock to the sides of the flask to detach still adherent cells and then add 7 ml of complete growth medium to neutralize the trypsin/EDTA solution.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Spin cells down at 1400 rpm for 4 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Resuspend the cell pellet in 10 ml DMEM complete growth medium by gently pipetting up and down.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Remove 1 ml of the cell suspension for cell counting and viability measurement.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Plate cells in a 96-well plate at a density of 0.02 × 106 cells/well in 100 μl DMEM complete medium. Note: Cells should be approximately 40–60% confluent when transfected.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 10. Incubate overnight in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: Day 2 morning: (e.g. Tuesday 11:00)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 1. ½ h to 1 h prior transfection aspirate old medium and add 100 μl OptiMEM reduced serum medium.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction:
2. Prepare plasmid DNA/PLUS solution and Lipofectamine™ LTX solution separately in OptiMEM in a volume ratio of 1:1, before combining the two solutions, according to the manufacturer’s instructions.
Total DNA: 0.1 μg/wellPLUS™ reagent: 0.1 μl/wellLipofectamine™ LTX: 0.5 μl/well.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: First dilute the plasmid DNA in OptiMEM, then add the PLUS™ reagent. Then dilute Lipofectamine™ LTX in OptiMEM and then transfer the plasmid DNA/PLUS solution to the Lipofectamine™ LTX solution.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Mix by pipetting up and down several times.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Incubate for 5 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Add 10 μl of the DNA/Lipofectamine mixture to each well and mix by shaking the plate carefully.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Incubate for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: Day 3 morning: (e.g. Wednesday 11:00)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Prepare compound dilutions in DMEM with 5% charcoal stripped FBS in 2-fold concentration shortly prior to use.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Aspirate old medium from the plate and add 50 μl of DMEM with charcoal stripped FBS to each well. Then add 50 μl of the prepared compound dilutions. The final volume is 100 μl/well and onefold compound concentration.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Incubate for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: Day 4 morning: (e.g. Thursday 11:00)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Prepare Luciferase lysis buffer: 4.8 ml H2Odd, 1.2 ml 5x lysis buffer, 6 μl CoA (coenzyme A trilithium salt, 270 mM), 6 μl DTT (dithiothreitol, 1 M)

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Aspirate old medium and add 50 μl luciferase lysis buffer/well. Freeze at − 80 °C to enhance cell lysis for at least 1 h and max. up to 1 week.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 3. For measurement, remove the plate from the freezer and let it thaw (app. ½ h).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Shake it for 5 min. Then transfer 40 μl lysed cell solution to a black 96-well plate. Shake the plate briefly (1 min.) to prevent bubbles within the wells.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #3: Luciferase Reporter Gene Assay with Caco-2 Cells
steps_raw.instruction: 5. The plate is now ready for measurement. Measure fluorescence first, at an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and an emission wavelength of 520 nm, if EGFP is used as internal control. After the injection of ATP (50 μl) and luciferin (50 μl) solutions measure luminescence. Normalize the luciferase-derived luminescence to the EGFP-derived fluorescence in order to account for differences in cell numbers.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Cells should be cultivated and fully differentiated on translucent filter inserts as described in Protocol #2. Before starting the experiments, measure TEER in order to assure the integrity of the cell monolayer. Note: Volumes are given for 12-well plates with inserts from Sarstedt (growth area = 1.1 cm2).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 1 before lunchtime (e.g. Monday 11:30):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS, serum-free DMEM and DMEM with 0.5% BSA in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Wash both the well and the filter insert at least once with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Put 0.5 ml of serum-free DMEM into each filter insert. Put 1.6 ml of DMEM with 0.5% BSA into each well.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Incubate for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 2 before lunchtime (e.g. Tuesday 11:30):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS and serum-free DMEM in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Prepare compound dilutions in serum-free DMEM in 2-fold concentration shortly prior to use.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Wash at least once with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Put 0.25 ml serum-free DMEM into the filter inserts. Fill the wells with 1.6 ml of serum-free DMEM.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Then add 0.25 ml of the respective compound dilutions into the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Incubate for 48 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 4 morning (e.g. Thursday 9:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Start with the Preparation of Stock Solutions of Substances for Micelles

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Note: Micelles simulating postprandial conditions contain cholesterol, sodium taurocholate, lysophosphatidylcholine, oleic acid and 2-oleoylglycerol. Final concentrations of these substances should be as follows: cholesterol 0.05 mM, sodium taurocholate 2 mM, lysophosphatidylcholine 0.2 mM, oleic acid 0.6 mM and 2-oleoylglycerol 0.2 mM [35, 36].

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction:
Note: As treatment and application of micelles is required in parallel, the substances mentioned above are prepared in 2-fold concentration and then diluted by the treatment to onefold. To prepare these 2-fold concentrations, stock solutions are prepared first [35, 36], with the concentrations stated in Table 1.
Table 1Components for micelles and concentrations [35, 36] of the stock solutionsSubstance2-fold concentrationConcentration of stock solutionCholesterol0.1 mM25 mMSodium taurocholate4 mM24 mMLysophosphatidylcholine0.4 mM100 mMOleic acid1.2 mM100 mM2-Oleoylglycerol0.4 mM100 mM

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Prepare stock solutions in chloroform:methanol 2:1 (v/v), except for the sodium taurocholate stock solution, which is prepared in serum-free DMEM.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Pipet the required amount of each stock solution, except for the sodium taurocholate stock solution, into a sterile glass vial with a screw-cap.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Then add the radioactively labelled cholesterol. The required final onefold activity is 1 μCi/ml (37 kBq/ml).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Evaporate this mixture to dryness under a gentle stream of nitrogen.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Add the required amount of sodium taurocholate stock solution and fill up with serum-free DMEM to achieve the desired final volume for 2-fold concentration.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Vortex the mixture vigorously for 5 min and then place it under a heating hood set at 37 °C on an orbital shaker (set at 100 rpm) for 1 h.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 7. In the meantime, prepare treatment and serum-free DMEM containing 1% human plasma (serves as acceptor on the basolateral side).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Filter sterilize the micellar solution through a 0.45 μm cellulose acetate filter.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Aspirate old medium from the plate. Put 1.6 ml of DMEM with human plasma into the wells. Put 0.25 ml micellar solution into the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 10. Then pipet 0.25 ml of compound treatment into the filter inserts. Note: Treatment shall be carried out for 48 h and then micelles and fresh treatment shall be added for another 2 h. Make sure to be on time.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 11. Place the plate on an orbital shaker (set at 200 rpm) in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 2 h.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 12. Hereafter, carefully aspirate the basolateral and apical medium. Then wash at least two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 13. Lyse the cells with 150 μl NP40 lysis buffer/insert for 30 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 14. Scratch the cells to the edge of the filter insert with the help of an inoculation needle. Be careful not to rip the filter membrane.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 15. Transfer the cell lysates into centrifuge tubes. Centrifuge the tubes at 13000 rpm at 4 °C for 20 min.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 16. After centrifuging, transfer the supernatants into new centrifuge tubes.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 17. Fill scintillation vials with 3.5 ml scintillation liquid and appropriately mark them.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 18. Pipet 50 μl of each cell lysate into a separate scintillation vial. As a control, pipet 250 μl of micellar solution that was applied to the inserts into a scintillation vial.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 19. Then measure dpm (disintegrations per minute) with the help of a liquid scintillation counter.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 5 (e. g.Friday):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #4: Cholesterol Uptake Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Perform a Bradford assay or equivalent in order to determine protein concentration in the cell lysates. This is required for expressing the results as dpm/mg protein or nmol cholesterol/mg protein.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Cells should be cultivated and fully differentiated on translucent filter inserts as described in Protocol #2. Before starting the experiments, measure TEER in order to assure the integrity of the cell monolayer. Note: Volumes are given for 12-well plates with inserts from Sarstedt (growth area = 1.1 cm2).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 1 morning (e.g. Sunday 10:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS, serum-free DMEM and DMEM with 0.5% BSA in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Wash both the well and the filter insert at least once with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Put 0.5 ml of serum-free DMEM into each filter insert. Put 1.6 ml of DMEM with 0.5% BSA into each well.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Incubate for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 2 morning (e.g. Monday 08:30):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Prepare stock solutions and micelles, as detailed in Protocol #4.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Filter sterilize the micellar solution through a 0.45 μm cellulose acetate filter.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Aspirate old medium from the plate. Wash cells once with PBS. Put 1.6 ml of serum-free DMEM into the wells and 0.25 ml of serum-free DMEM into the inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Then pipet 0.25 ml of the micellar solution into the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Place the plate on an orbital shaker (200 rpm) in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 3 morning (e.g. Tuesday 10:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm PBS and serum-free DMEM in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Prepare compound dilutions in serum-free DMEM in 2-fold concentration shortly prior to use.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Wash cells once with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Put 0.25 ml serum-free DMEM into the filter inserts. Fill the wells with 1.6 ml of serum-free DMEM.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Then add 0.25 ml of the respective compound dilutions into the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Incubate for 48 h on an orbital shaker (200 rpm) in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 5 morning (e.g. Thursday 08:30):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm serum-free DMEM in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Prepare serum-free DMEM containing 1% human plasma (serves as acceptor on the basolateral side).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Prepare micelles as described for day 2, but without adding any cholesterol.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Put 0.25 ml serum-free DMEM into the filter inserts. Fill the wells with 1.6 ml of serum-free DMEM containing human plasma. Then pipet 0.25 ml of the micellar solution into the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Incubate for 24 h on an orbital shaker (set at 200 rpm) in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: Day 6 morning (e.g. Friday 10:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Carefully collect the apical and basolateral supernatants by pipetting them off and put them into 2.0 ml centrifuge tubes.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Centrifuge for 5 min at 13000 rpm at 4 °C. Transfer the supernatants into new centrifuge tubes.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Wash the cells two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 4. If you want to show the amount of cholesterol remaining in the cells, lyse the cells with 150 μl NP40 lysis buffer/insert for 30 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Scratch the cells to the edge of the filter insert with the help of an inoculation needle. Be careful not to rip the filter membrane.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Transfer the cell lysates into centrifuge tubes. Centrifuge the tubes at 13000 rpm at 4 °C for 20 min.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 7. After centrifuging, transfer the supernatants into new centrifuge tubes.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Fill scintillation vials with 3.5 ml scintillation liquid and appropriately mark them.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Pipet 50 μl of each cell lysate, 250 μl of each apical supernatant and 800 μl of each basolateral supernatant into a separate scintillation vial.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 10. Then measure dpm (disintegrations per minute) with the help of a liquid scintillation counter.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #5: Cholesterol Efflux Assay
steps_raw.instruction: 11. Perform a Bradford assay or equivalent in order to determine protein concentration in the cell lysates. Normalize the counts in the cell lysates to the protein content, if you want to show the amount of cholesterol remaining in the cells. In the final analysis, consider that the volumes of the apical supernatant, the basolateral supernatant and the cell lysate are unequal and include a factor accounting for this disparity.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: Cells should be cultivated and fully differentiated on transparent filter inserts as described in Protocol #2. Before starting the experiments, measure TEER in order to assure the integrity of the cell monolayer. Note: Volumes are given for 12-well plates with inserts from Sarstedt (growth area = 1.1 cm2).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: Day 1 and Day 2 of this protocol are identical to Supplementary Protocol #5 (Additional file 1).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: Day 4 morning (e.g. Thursday 09:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Wash cells two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Fix cells with 3.7% formaldehyde in PBS for 15 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Aspirate formalin and wash cells two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Permeabilize cells with 0.2% Triton X in PBS for 10 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Wash cells two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Add blocking solution (5% BSA in PBS) to both the insert and the well for 6 h. Put the plate at 4 °C.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Prepare a dilution of the primary antibody in PBS with 1% BSA according to the manufacturer’s instructions (ABCG5: 2 μg/ml (1150), NPC1L1: 10 μg/ml (1:100), ABCB1: 2 μg/ml (1100)).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Wash two times with PBS. Add 500 μl of the primary antibody working solution to the inserts and incubate overnight at 4 °C.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: Day 5 morning (e.g. Friday 09:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Prepare a dilution of the secondary antibody in PBS with 1% BSA according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Alexa Fluor 488 conjugated goat anti-rabbit antibody: 4 μg/ml (1400)).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Wash two times with PBS. Add 500 μl of the secondary antibody working solution to the inserts and incubate for 3 h at 4 °C in the dark.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Prepare a dilution of DAPI in PBS with 0.2% BSA (1 μg/ml (11000)).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Wash two times with PBS. Add 500 μl of the DAPI working solution to the inserts and incubate for 15 min at room temperature in the dark.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Wash two times with PBS.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Put one drop of mounting medium on a microscope slide.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Then carefully cut the filter membrane from the plastic insert with the help of a scalpel. Carefully place the membrane upside down in the mounting medium, avoiding air bubbles.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Put one drop of mounting medium onto the membrane and then cover it with a cover slip, again avoiding air bubbles.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Put the microscope slides into a box and store the box at 4 °C in the dark while the mounting medium is drying off.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #6: Determination of Protein Level of Major Cholesterol Transporters - Confocal Microscopy for ABCG5, NPC1L1 and ABCB1
steps_raw.instruction: Samples are now ready for inspection with the confocal laser scanning microscope.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: Cells should be cultivated and fully differentiated on transparent filter inserts as described in Protocol #2. Before starting the experiments, measure TEER in order to assure the integrity of the cell monolayer. Note: Volumes are given for 12-well plates with inserts from Sarstedt (growth area = 1.1 cm2).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: Day 1 and Day 2 of this protocol are identical to Supplementary Protocol #5 (Additional file 1).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: Day 4 morning (e.g. Thursday 09:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Pre-warm serum-free DMEM in a 37 °C water bath.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Prepare a dilution of resazurin in serum-free DMEM at 10 μg/ml concentration.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Carefully aspirate medium from both the wells and the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Wash at least once with serum-free DMEM.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Add 0.5 ml of the resazurin dilution to the filter inserts and 1.6 ml to the wells.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 6. Incubate for 2 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 7. Using forceps, carefully transfer the filter inserts into a new 12-well plate.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 8. Remove 400 μl of apical medium from each filter insert and pipet it into the wells of a 48-well plate.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 9. Measure fluorescence on a plate reader, using an excitation of 535 nm and an emission of 590 nm.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 10. Carefully aspirate remaining resazurin dilution from the filter inserts.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 11. Add 500 μl of crystal violet solution per insert and incubate for 10 min at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 12. Using forceps, pull the insert out of the well one by one and carefully wash it with deionized water. Repeat this washing step till the water is colorless (3–5 times).

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 13. Carefully aspirate remaining water from both the inserts and the wells and let the inserts dry overnight at room temperature.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: Day 5 morning (e.g. Friday 09:00):

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Add 500 μl sodium citrate:ethanol per insert.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 2. Carefully shake for 10 s.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 3. Add another 500 μl sodium citrate:ethanol per insert.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 4. Again shake for 10 s.

steps_raw.title: Protocol #7: Viability Assays - Resazurin Conversion Assay and Subsequent Crystal Violet Staining
steps_raw.instruction: 5. Pipet 100 μl out of each insert into a 96-well plate and measure OD at 595 nm.

steps_raw.title: Statistical Analysis
steps_raw.instruction: GraphPad Prism™ 6.01 was used for statistical analysis and the generation of figures. Differences between two groups were analyzed by a two-tailed paired t-test. Multiple comparisons were carried out by one-way ANOVA with Dunnett post-test. P < 0.05 was considered as statistically significant.

materials: Caco-2 cells were obtained from DSMZ (Braunschweig, Germany). All chemicals were purchased from Carl Roth (Karlsruhe, Germany) or Sigma-Aldrich (Vienna, Austria), unless otherwise stated. PET translucent and transparent filter inserts (both 0.4 μM pore size) for 12-well plates, 12-well companion plates and 75 cm2 flasks were obtained from Sarstedt (Nuembrecht, Germany). EMEM, DMEM, L-glutamine, penicillin/streptomycin mixture (potassium penicillin/streptomycin sulfate) and 100x NEAA were purchased from Lonza Group Ltd. (Basel, Switzerland). FBS and trypsin were obtained from Gibco via Invitrogen (Lofer, Austria). Human plasma was obtained from healthy, young volunteers. OptiMEM was acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Vienna, Austria). D-[2-14C]-mannitol, [1,2-3H(N)]-cholesterol, liquid scintillation cocktail (Ultima Gold™) and liquid scintillation vials (MidiVials) were obtained from Perkin Elmer (Traiskirchen, Austria). SYBR® Green I Master and cOmplete™ protease inhibitor cocktail were purchased from Roche Diagnostics (Vienna, Austria). FuGENE® HD transfection reagent and reporter lysis 5x buffer were acquired from Promega (Mannheim, Germany). Luciferin was purchased from Synchem (Felsburg, Germany). TRIzol, RNase-free glycogen, Phasemaker™ tubes, the High Capacity cDNA Reverse Transcription Kit and the RNase inhibitor were obtained from Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher Scientific via Life Tech Austria (Vienna, Austria). The peqGOLD total RNA kit was purchased from VWR (Vienna, Austria). Lipofectamine™ LTX with PLUS™ reagent was purchased from Fisher Scientific (Vienna, Austria). LPEI (linear polyethylenimine) transfection reagent was kindly provided by Prof. Manfred Ogris (Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, University of Vienna, Vienna, Austria). Fluoromount™ aqueous mounting medium, cholesterol, L-α-lysophosphatidylcholine type I (from egg yolk), oleic acid, sodium taurocholate hydrate and Dextran blue (from Leuconostoc spp.) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Vienna, Austria). Precision cover slips (Ø 18 mm, borosilicate glass, 0.17 ± 0.005 mm) and microscope slides were obtained from Carl Roth (Karlsruhe, Germany). 2-Oleoylglycerol was purchased from Cayman Chemical via Sanova (Vienna, Austria). The EGFP expression plasmid (pEGFP-N1) was purchased from Clontech Laboratories (Mountain View, CA, USA). The hLXRα (human LXRα, full length; pCMV) and the hLXRβ (human LXRβ, full length; pcDNA3.1+) expression plasmids were obtained from Missouri S&T cDNA Resource Center (Rolla, MO, USA). The ABCA1_Luc (Luciferase reporter vector driven by the ABCA1 promoter; pGL4.14) was kindly provided by Dr. Ira G. Schulman (University of Virginia Health System, Charlottesville, VA, USA) [81]. The hLXRα_Gal4 (chimeric expression of hLXRα-LBD/Gal4-DBD; pCMX), hLXRβ_Gal4 (chimeric expression of hLXRβ-LBD/Gal4-DBD; pCMX) and the UAS_Luc (Gal4 responsive Luciferase reporter vector; pTK-MH100x4-Luc) plasmids were kindly provided by Prof. Makoto Makishima (Nihon University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan). The primary antibody against ABCA1 (polyclonal, rabbit, #NB400–105) was purchased from Novus Biologicals (Vienna, Austria). The primary antibody against Actin (monoclonal, mouse, #691001) was acquired from MP Biomedicals via Fisher Scientific Austria (Vienna, Austria). The horseradish-peroxidase conjugated anti-rabbit secondary antibody (#7074) as well as the horseradish-peroxidase conjugated anti-mouse secondary antibody (#7076) were purchased from Cell Signaling via New England Biolabs (Frankfurt am Main, Germany). The Alexa Fluor 594 conjugated anti-ZO-1 antibody (monoclonal, mouse, #339194), the primary antibodies against ABCG5 (polyclonal, rabbit, #PA5–53439), NPC1L1 (polyclonal, rabbit, #PA1–16800) and ABCB1 (polyclonal, rabbit, #PA5–82006) and the Alexa Fluor 488 conjugated goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody (polyclonal, #A-11008) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Vienna, Austria). ABCA1 (Hs_ABCA1_1_SG QuantiTect Primer Assay), ABCG5 (Hs_ABCG5_1_SG QuantiTect Primer Assay), NPC1L1 (Hs_NPC1L1_1_SG QuantiTect Primer Assay), ABCB1 (Hs_ABCB1_1_SG QuantiTect Primer Assay) and GAPDH (Hs_GAPDH_1_SG QuantiTect Primer Assay) primers were purchased from Qiagen (Vienna, Austria). Stock solutions of the compounds were prepared in DMSO, except for digitonin, which was dissolved in ethanol. Final concentrations of DMSO did not exceed 0.1%, and final concentrations of ethanol did not exceed 1%.

Complete growth medium consisted of EMEM, supplemented with 20% heat-inactivated FBS, 2 mM L-glutamine and 1x NEAA. Cryopreservation medium was composed of 70% EMEM, 20% heat-inactivated FBS and 10% DMSO. Apical medium consisted of EMEM, supplemented with 2 mM L-glutamine, 1x NEAA, 100 U ml− 1 penicillin and 100 μg ml− 1 streptomycin. Basolateral medium was composed of EMEM, supplemented with 20% heat-inactivated FBS, 2 mM L-glutamine, 1x NEAA, 100 U ml− 1 penicillin and 100 μg ml− 1 streptomycin. Serum-free DMEM was DMEM medium supplemented with 2 mM L-glutamine, 1x NEAA, 100 U ml− 1 penicillin and 100 μg ml− 1 streptomycin. DMEM with 0.5% BSA was serum-free DMEM mentioned above, additionally supplemented with 0.5% BSA. DMEM complete growth medium consisted of DMEM medium supplemented with 20% heat-inactivated FBS, 2 mM L-glutamine, 1x NEAA, 100 U ml− 1 penicillin and 100 μg ml− 1 streptomycin. DMEM with charcoal stripped FBS was serum-free DMEM mentioned above, additionally supplemented with 5% charcoal stripped FBS.

PBS (phosphate buffered saline, pH 7.4) contained 123 mM NaCl, 10.4 mM Na2HPO4, 3.16 mM KH2PO4. The NP40 lysis buffer consisted of ddH2O containing 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM HEPES and 1% NP40. The pH was adjusted to 7.4. The 3x sample buffer contained 187.5 mM TRIS-HCl (pH 6.8), 0.2 M SDS, 30% glycerol and 0.2 mM bromophenol blue. The 3x sample buffer containing β-mercaptoethanol consisted of 85% 3x sample buffer and 15% β-mercaptoethanol. TBST (pH 8.0) was composed of 25 mM TRIS-base, 190 mM NaCl and 0.1% Tween-20 in ddH2O. The ECL reagent consisted of 100 mM TRIS-base (pH 8.5), 1.24 mM luminol, 0.2 mM p-coumaric acid and 0.009% H2O2 in ddH2O. The trypsin/EDTA mixture contained 0.05% trypsin and 0.02% Na2EDTA. The crystal violet solution was composed of 1% crystal violet in 20% methanol and was filtered before use. The sodium citrate:ethanol solution consisted of 0.05 M sodium citrate in 50% ethanol. The ATP solution contained 20 mM tricine (pH 7.8), 21.5 mM MgCl2 and 3.8 mM adenosine-5′-triphosphate disodium salt. The luciferin solution consisted of 32 mg/ml luciferin and 21 mM tricine (pH 7.8).
